title: Designing multi-view activation sets
learningObjectives: Evaluate different strategies for grouping activations into semantic sets.
Compare SetConCA's semantic grouping to Temporal SAEs' temporal grouping and ConCA's ICA-based grouping.
Analyze the impact of 'invalid' sets on the contrastive learning signal.

whyThisLesson.text:
SetConCA relies entirely on the assumption that we can construct valid 'sets' of activations that share a conceptual core. If the sets are garbage, the contrastive loss will optimize for noise, and the features will be worse than a standard SAE.

In this lesson, we explore HOW to construct semantic sets from language model activations. We examine the trade-offs between entity-based grouping, syntactic grouping, and temporal windowing. We also compare our theoretical approach to recent related works like Temporal SAEs and ConCA to isolate SetConCA's unique contribution.

sections.bridgeFrom: We established that SetConCA uses a set-level contrastive loss to encourage feature purity. But where do these sets come from? We must define the criteria for grouping.

intuition.analogy: Creating a photo album for facial recognition training.
intuition.analogyLimit: Unlike photos which clearly contain faces, token activations contain deeply entangled semantic, syntactic, and positional information.
intuition.text:
Imagine training a facial recognition system. You need 'sets' of photos of the same person. If you group photos by the time they were taken (a temporal window), you might get pictures of Alice, then Bob, then a tree. A contrastive loss would try to find the 'shared identity' of Alice, Bob, and the tree, leading to complete failure.

However, if you meticulously group photos by the actual person in them (entity grouping), the contrastive loss works perfectly. The challenge in language models is that we don't have explicit 'faces'. We just have tokens. How do we group tokens so they genuinely share a concept without manually labeling them all?

toyExample.description: Evaluate 3 different set construction strategies on a synthetic corpus.

steps.action: Strategy A: Group identical entities (e.g., all tokens referring to 'Paris').
steps.result: Captures a strong semantic concept ('Paris'), but requires complex coreference resolution.
steps.insight: Entity-based sets are highly semantic but hard to construct at scale.

steps.action: Strategy B: Group by temporal window (consecutive tokens).
steps.result: Captures local context and syntax, but mixes distinct semantic entities.
steps.insight: Temporal sets are easy to build but may encourage syntactic over semantic features.

steps.result: Produces a contrastive signal that completely disrupts the dictionary.
steps.insight: A bad set definition produces features that are WORSE than a pointwise SAE.

visual.question: How do different grouping strategies align with underlying concepts?
visual.description: A visualization showing a sequence of text with colored highlights representing different grouping strategies, compared against ground-truth concept labels.

technical.text: Constructing multi-view activation sets requires balancing signal purity against extraction scalability. Strategy 1 (Same entity) groups tokens like 'Paris' across different contexts. Strategy 2 (Syntactic role) groups subject nouns. Strategy 3 (Temporal window) groups consecutive tokens. Strategy 4 (Topic cluster) groups tokens from the same document. Strategy 5 (Coreference) groups pronouns and nouns referring to the same entity locally.
definitions: Temporal SAEs (Bhalla et al. 2024): An architecture that uses consecutive tokens as views, relying on a temporal rather than semantic grouping.
ConCA (Liu et al. 2025): A method that decomposes activations into concept components using ICA-inspired statistical methods rather than explicit set construction.
Invalid Set: A grouping of tokens that do not share any meaningful semantic or structural concept, introducing noise into the contrastive objective.

mathematics.label: Set Purity Metric

symbols.c_i: Ground-truth concept vector for token i

mathematics.plainExplanation: The purity of a set is the average semantic similarity between all pairs of tokens in the set.
mathematics.whyNeeded: To evaluate the quality of our set construction strategy before running the expensive SetConCA training.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without a measure of set purity, we fly blind and cannot diagnose why SetConCA might fail.
mathematics.encourages: Designing grouping strategies that maximize true semantic overlap.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the model will actually learn those shared concepts if the reconstruction loss overpowers the contrastive loss.

evidence.claim: Strategy 1 (same entity) provides the strongest concept signal but requires robust entity resolution.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: Grouping by explicit entities yields high concept purity at the cost of implementation complexity.

evidence.claim: Strategy 3 (temporal) is easiest to implement but may capture syntactic rather than semantic similarity.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: Adjacent tokens are easy to group but often share structural rather than meaning-based traits.

failureModes.name: Spurious Correlation Capture
failureModes.mechanism: The set construction inadvertently groups tokens by an unintended variable (e.g., all entities happen to be at the start of a sentence).
failureModes.symptom: Features learned by SetConCA represent positional or syntactic artifacts rather than semantic concepts.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Test the features on identical entities placed in highly diverse syntactic positions.
failureModes.mitigation: Ensure diversity within the constructed sets to decorrelate the target concept from spurious variables.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Critical for ensuring the contrastive loss targets the right level of abstraction.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Set construction is the primary data engineering challenge for SetConCA.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Selecting semantic over temporal grouping to enforce concept purity.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Train SetConCA using entity-based sets vs. temporal sets and compare the resulting feature interpretability.

exercises.prompt: If you group tokens using a naive temporal window (like Temporal SAEs), what kind of features might SetConCA amplify?
exercises.reveal: It will likely amplify features related to local syntactic structures, n-grams, or sequential flow, rather than abstract, cross-context semantic concepts.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Why is it hypothesized that a bad set definition (e.g., random tokens) produces features WORSE than a standard pointwise SAE?
rubric: Explains that standard SAEs only have to reconstruct.
Explains that random sets force the contrastive loss to pull together unrelated activations.
Concludes that this actively distorts the geometry, destroying even the baseline pointwise structures.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: A pointwise SAE reconstructs data based on local geometry. If you add a contrastive loss with random sets, you actively penalize the model for maintaining accurate local geometry, forcing it to cluster activations that have nothing in common. This destroys the useful structure the SAE would have naturally learned.

bridgeToNext.text: We've discussed the theory of the objective and the construction of sets. Next, we design a controlled, realistic toy experiment to test these hypotheses rigorously.
